title: Scientist Stories: Eric Lander, Forefront of Genomics - YouTube

foreign
Lander I'm the director of the broad
Institute of MIT and Harvard how did you
first get involved in the Human Genome
Project
oh how did I first get involved in the
Human Genome Project well
if I go back I met David botstein in the
spring of 1985 and I had been working in
well I've been on the faculty of the
Harvard Business School teaching
managerial economics while Moonlighting
learning molecular biology in my spare
time working in flies and worms
but that particular day after the
Tuesday colloquium at MIT Barbara Meyer
who introduced me to David botstein in
the way I hated to be introduced here's
this mathematician who's learning
biology because of course most
biologists ran the other way when you
said anything that involved
mathematician in it but Barbara knew
wherever she spoke because botstein
immediately cottoned onto the idea that
this was a mathematician because
bottstein in 1980 had published this
paper about
mapping human disease genes with
restriction fragment length
polymorphisms and he was frustrated that
he was unable to apply it Beyond Simple
mendelian traits
and he'd wanted to talk to some person
who knew mathematics and genetics but he
hadn't really managed to interest any
but find anybody interested in anybody
so botstein immediately like latched on
and started talking about this question
of could we ever map complex traits
polygenic traits and we we had so much
fun arguing about that David said you
couldn't do things and I said you could
do things he's from the Bronx I'm from
Brooklyn we had a we had a great time
arguing we had so much fun we argued
again the next day and then we went to
um he had to give a talk at the human
gene mapping five meeting in in Helsinki
Finland but he hadn't written anything
else or done any other research since
his 1980 paper and so he really didn't
know what he was going to give his
keynote on so we worked madly for
several months on new ways to map more
complex traits and David gave a talk on
this he invited me to Helsinki that was
my first ever exposure to the human
genetics community in the human gene
mapping Community I came back and then
David Baltimore gave me a position does
a whitehead fellow while I was still
teaching at Harvard and
sometime in the early spring of 1986 I
got an invitation to Cold Spring Harbor
to attend the 1986 Symposium on the the
molecular biology of homo sapiens the
famous Symposium were the Human Genome
Project was debated and I attended I
gave a talk in the first session I think
the third Talk of the meeting and then
they had the famous
debate on should we have a Human Genome
Project
and how did I get involved in the Human
Genome Project I raised my hand twice
during the debate and
made comments from the point of view of
a young scientist and what I was you
know what the issues were to to for
thinking about this project and
um
Maxine singer and Paul Berg pulled me
over afterwards as we're all leaving and
says oh those were really interesting
comments can we uh go have dinner
together at Blackford at Cold Spring
Harbor and we sat down and we did and we
talked and then a couple months later
uh a meeting was getting organized not
enough possible
Human Genome Project and they realized
they didn't have anybody knew anything
about data and Analysis and computation
and so they said oh it was that guy at
Cold Spring Harbor who raised his hand
in the in the debate and so I got
invited to kind of co-chair that thing
and anyway through a series of
incredibly lucky accidents of meeting
David botstein who wanted to talk about
human genetics and going to the 1986
Cold Spring Harbor meeting and as I
always tell my students raise your hand
raising my hand
I then got
just sort of drawn in to this nascent
idea of a Genome Project in 1986 which
was still
very ill-formed it was still Wally
Gilbert saying let's put up a big
Factory and sequence everything and and
uh using Southern blots and whatever and
and there were all the tensions and
David botstein and David Baltimore being
very skeptical so through no planning
whatsoever I stumbled into this
incredible ferment and it changed my
life
why was sequencing the whole Human
Genome important
The genome
I think traces intellectually most
strongly back to David botz Line's 1980
paper
which said if you could build a whole
genome genetic map of polymorphisms you
could use its Trace inheritance for any
mendelian disorder
that was the first time biology had
hypothesis free
discovery
everything else in biology the the
cartoon story is you have a hypothesis
and you test your hypothesis but nobody
ever asked the children where do
hypotheses come from and and what if
you're not smart enough to have
hypothesis of what causes Cystic
Fibrosis or Huntington's disease
what was amazing about genetic mapping
and of course known to people who did
flies and worms but not known to human
geneticists is if you could do genetic

without knowing what a gene was
well that necessarily meant we needed
the whole genome because we weren't
looking for anything in particular we
needed markers everywhere we needed all

seriously the botsline idea of
mendelian mapping a mapping of mendelian

there were attempts to kind of cut
Corners
um there were you know even later Craig

and everything when we can't even really

meeting I guess a chronic the genes for
chronic granulomatous disease and
deschan muscular dystrophy had been
found and were reported and they looked

a kind of view that well we shouldn't go
try to get more genes till we could
understand these genes and it's really
worth looking at that paragraph near the
end of the this News and Views
and that was a perfect example of kind
of old style biology thinking which hung
on for decades but was an old view that
to understand something you had to pick
one example and go deep
and that's great that's one way to
understand things but what the genome
taught us was that there were
complementary ways that you could get
aerial views and learn things that you
would never learn by focusing individual
on individual things that if you
um
if you were a geologist and you looked

could learn a lot about that rock or
outcropping but on the other hand you
would miss huge big pictures whole
layers that existed in many parts of the
world or continental Drift from the
continents fitting together and so it
was the moment in which I think biology
came to grips with the fact that the
pink picture taught you things that the
little picture didn't then vice versa
what was your perspective on NIH
directing the hgp
well I guess we can now say you know why
was it a good idea that it was NIH

gets credit for having floated this idea

the most important thing was it was it

that was to me and that's intellectual

botstein used to joke that

that

and I sort of shot a hole in that by

scale is only makes sense if the labs

the recognition that problems have a

that was the natural skill for this

you'd never do it one group not so good
because then you can go home at five

we've come to understand since that
there are problems that have different

what were the early rate limiting

sequencing was not even
you know a serious
consideration at scale getting a
megabase thumb
was like a huge

and there came to be an understanding

event of

half a superconducting super collider
